docs.title: Documentation
docs.text: This file explains the standards used in this repository, as well as further tips and advice.

docs.title: Challenge Authors
docs.text: If you are making a challenge, please refer to the Challenge Format page for relevant documentation.

docs.title: Repository Structure
docs.text: For information on the structure of a challenge repository, please refer to the Repository Structure page.

docs.title: checker.py
docs.text: For information on using the checker.py deployment and testing utility, please refer to the checker.py page.

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text: Below, we go through the challenge format, including the structure of the challenge, the expected files, and the expected content of these files.

docs.title: Challenge Format
docs.text:
A challenge should have at least the following files and directories:

  • challenge.toml is a TOML file that contains all the metadata for the challenge.
  • README.md is a markdown file that contains an informal overview of the challenge;
  • Source/, which contains all the source files for the challenge;
  • Handout/, which contains all the files that are given to the contestants;

Additionally, Tests/ and/or Build/ directories may be present, where the Tests/ directory contains any tests for the challenge, and the Build/ directory contains any build scripts for the challenge.

The challenge directory itself should be put in a category directory of the desired category, see Categories for more detail.

docs.title: challenge.toml
docs.text: The challenge.toml file describes all the needed metadata for the challenge, for both the running and presenting of the challenge. The following fields are supported in the challenge.toml file:

docs.title: UUID
docs.text:
The first line of the challenge.toml file should be a UUIDv4, as this is used to uniquely identify the challenge. On linux, you can generate a random UUIDv4 with the command uuidgen.

[5da55141-2f35-4822-91b5-fd28e1686d09]
# rest of file

docs.title: Name
docs.text:
The name of the challenge, this is what will be displayed to the contestants on the CTF platform.

name = "Example Challenge"

docs.title: Flag
docs.text:
The flag for the challenge, with an assigned static point value.

flag = { "FLAG{example_flag}" = 100 }

docs.title: Difficulty
docs.text:
The difficulty of the challenge, usually limited to "easy", "medium", and "hard".

difficulty = "medium"

docs.title: Description
docs.text:
The description of the challenge, this is what will be displayed to the contestants on the CTF platform.

description = """Example description line1
Example description line 2"""
# or
description = """Example description single line"""

docs.title: URL
docs.text:
The connection string of the challenge, as e.g. http://{{IP}}:{{PORT}} or nc {{IP}} {{PORT}}. It should contain {{IP}} and {{PORT}} placeholders that will be replaced with the actual IP or URL and port when the challenge is run.

Optional The URL field should be omitted if the challenge doesn't need to be run.

url = "http://{{IP}}:{{PORT}}"

docs.title: Instanced
docs.text:
A boolean value indicating whether the challenge is instanced.

Optional This field should be omitted if the challenge is not instanced, and and defaults to false. challenge is instanced.

instanced = true

docs.title: Hints
docs.text:
A list of hints for the challenge with their point cost, shown on the CTF platform.

Optional If omitted, no hints will be shown for the challenge.

hints = { "Example hint 1" = 10, "Example hint 2" = 20 }

docs.title: Hidden
docs.text:
A boolean value indicating whether the challenge is hidden. Hidden challenges are not shown to contestants on the CTF platform, but are still deployed.

Optional This field should be omitted if the challenge is not hidden, and defaults to false.

hidden = true

docs.title: Dynamic Flag
docs.text:
TODO

Optional Defaults to false.

dynamic_flag = true

docs.title: Tags
docs.text:
A list of tags for the challenge, shown on the CTF platform.

Optional If omitted, no tags will be shown for the challenge.

tags = ["example"]

docs.title: Example challenge.toml
docs.text:
[1457f303-0894-42c9-bd06-484e92d8fc5d]
name = "Example"
flag = { "FLAG{example_flag}" = 100 }
difficulty = "medium"
description = '''
This is an example challenge.
'''
url = "nc {{IP}} {{PORT}}"
instanced = true
hints = { "Try harder" = 10 }
# hidden, dynamic_flag, and tags are omitted in this example

docs.title: README.md
docs.text: The README contains an informal overview of the challenge, including any important deployment information, design notes, high-level solution, and other considerations. This may contain sensitive information.

docs.title: Source Directory
docs.text: This directory contains all the needed source files for the challenge, this is where the Dockerfile and any other server hosted code should be.

docs.title: Default Deployment
docs.text:
By default, the checker.py program will attempt to build, run, and destroy the challenge purely off the Dockerfile in the source folder. This means that, if your challenge has a Dockerfile that works with a simple docker build and docker run command, you don't need to add any additional scripts to the source folder, and the checker.py will handle the deployment for you based on the format specified in the challenge.toml.

Of course, as specified in the challenge.toml section, if your challenge doesn't need to be run then you just omit the url field in the challenge.toml file.

docs.title: Additional deployment configuration
docs.text: However, if you require additional deployment configuration, you can add two scripts to the source folder, run.sh and destroy.sh.

docs.title: run.sh
docs.text:
The run.sh should be a shell script that handles the running of the challenge. The run.sh script is called as follows:

run.sh --hostname HOSTNAME --port PORT --flag FLAG --registry REGISTRY

docs.title: HOSTNAME
docs.text: HOSTNAME is the hostname that will be provided to the players, this may be an IP or DNS address.

docs.title: PORT
docs.text: PORT is the port where the challenge is to be run. Note that, whilst the invoking checker.py bears the responsibility of the port being available, run.sh is responsible for ensuring the port is accessible on the provided hostname.

docs.title: FLAG
docs.text: FLAG is an optional argument that passes the flag specified in the challenge.toml to the run.sh script, this can be used to deploy dynamic flags. If your challenge does not support dynamic flags, you can simply ignore this argument and deploy the static flag as specified in the challenge.toml.

docs.title: REGISTRY
docs.text: REGISTRY is a docker registry that should be used to push any built images to, in case docker service is supported.

docs.title: destroy.sh
docs.text: The destroy.sh is a shell script that ensures the deployment is destroyed. The script should exit silently with code 0 if a deployment is successfully destroyed, and print any errors and throw a tantrum otherwise.

docs.title: Handout Directory
docs.text: This is what gets given to the contestants, all files in this folder are zipped and then distributed.

docs.title: Build Directory
docs.text: TODO

docs.title: Tests Directory
docs.text: TODO

docs.title: checker.py
docs.text: TODO: needs to be updated

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
--challenges
--categories
--flags
--handouts
--check
--run
--test

docs.title: Challenges
docs.text: Lists all challenges present in the repository.

docs.title: Categories
docs.text: Lists all main and sub categories present in the repository.

docs.title: flags
docs.text: Lists all flags for all challenges present in the repository.

docs.title: Handouts
docs.text: Lists all handouts for all relevant challenges present in the repository.

docs.title: Check
docs.text: Ensures all challenges are properly formatted.

docs.title: Run
docs.text:
Takes an optional argument, if present it attempts to run a challenge by this name. If none is found the command fails, if the challenge is found and successfully started it returns a connection string to the challenge.

If no arguments are present it attempts to run all challenges. No connection string is given in this case.

When running the challenges the port range 4000-4999 will be used to allocate ports to each challenge. The order of port allocation is done sequentially, sorted by uuid.

docs.title: Test
docs.text: Exact same syntax as the Run command, however it will run tests on the provided challenge. If this no challenge is specified all challenges will be tested. The results will be written to STDOUT.

docs.title: Repository layout
docs.text:
TODO: needs to be updated

Here is an example challenge repository layout, all folder and filenames in this document are case-insensitive. However, it is recommended to follow the casing outlined here, should two paths be case-identical (eg Test/ and test/) then it is not defined which will be chosen.

.
| - Category_1/
|   | - Subcategory_1/
|   |   | - Challenge_1/
|   |   |   | - Handout/
|   |   |   |   \ challenge.c
|   |   |   | - Source/
|   |   |   |   | - run.sh
|   |   |   |   | - destroy.sh
|   |   |   |   | - Dockerfile
|   |   |   |   \ - challenge.c
|   |   |   | - Tests/
|   |   |   |   \ main.py
|   |   |   | - Build/
|   |   |   |   \ build.sh
|   |   |   | - challenge.toml
|   |   |   | - Description.md
|   |   |   \ - README.md
|   |   | - Description.md  
|   |   | - category.toml
|   |   \ - README.md
|   | - Subcategory_2/
|   |   | - Challenge_2/
|   |   \ - README.md
|   | - Challenge_3/
|   | - Banner.png
|   \ - README.md
| - checker.py
| - config.toml
\ - README.md

Note: not all folders are expanded, Challenge_2 and 3 are expected to have the same (base) structure as Challenge_1

docs.title: checker.py
docs.text: Checker.py is the command line utility for a challenge repository, it contains the functionality to check the validity of the challenges, list different aspects of all challenges, test challenges and run challenges.

docs.title: config.toml
docs.text:
This file is optional, and is only required when not running the challenge from an instancer. This file should contain:

  • The base name of the hosting server. This will be passed as the {{IP}} parameter in a challenge connection string.
  • A slack-compatible webhook URL, this is optional but errors will be reported to this endpoint.

docs.title: Category format
docs.text:
A category contains at least the following files.

README.md
Description.md
category.toml
Banner.png

docs.title: README.md
docs.text:
The README contains an informal description of the (sub) category. For example:

## PWN
The world of low level exploitation!

docs.title: Description.md
docs.text: The public description of the category, this is what players will see.

docs.title: category.toml
docs.text:
Contains the following information:

  • name
  • uuid
  • Path to banner image (optional)

An example toml file would be

name = "example category"
banner = "Banner.png"
uuid = "84c988fa-9f42-42c7-b28a-8202b0962798"

docs.title: Banner.png
docs.text: This is the banner image for the category, a 1024x1024 PNG image is recommended. The file name is not fixed, but instead has to be pointed to by category.toml
